title: #sales: 1769014420
preview:
Hannah Schmitt: Need quick help on EU data residency objection. Prospect (EU bank) wants “EU-only processing + EU-only storage + no US access” for Hosted. What’s the approved phrasing + what do we *not* promise? @Olga Petrov @Daniel Carter @Marcus Lin

Lukas Meyer: +1, similar objection came up in EMEA last week. They’re asking if *anything* touches US systems (logs, support tooling, etc). Want to keep this clean.

Avery Johnson: Same here on another deal. Can we get a pasteable paragraph + a “don’t say this” list?

Priyom Das: They also asked if model routing could ever fail over to a non-EU region under load. Need to confirm.

Marcus Lin: jumping in. First: do NOT promise “EU-only” unless we’re talking Private deployment w/ customer-controlled infra, or we have a written contract amendment that explicitly defines what’s in/out.

Marcus Lin: For Hosted: we can support region selection (incl EU) for request processing, but we should be precise that some operational metadata may be processed/stored outside the selected region depending on the system (billing, abuse prevention, support). We need to use the approved language.

Naomi Feldman: +1. Also: don’t imply “no failover ever.” Our default is to keep traffic in-region if you’ve selected EU, but we can’t blanket guarantee “never leaves EU under any condition” unless it’s explicitly in the commercial terms / Private. Say “region-pinned” and “no cross-region routing by default for customer content” (but avoid absolute words like “never”).

Hannah Schmitt: got it. What’s the exact approved snippet? They’re literally asking for “contractual commitment that all data incl logs stays in EU and only EU staff can access.”

Olga Petrov: Here’s what we can say (approved style). Use “Customer Content” vs “data” to avoid ambiguity. Also do not commit on “EU-only personnel” access.

Olga Petrov: Pasteable response (Hosted + EU region):
“Redwood Inference supports regional processing for Hosted API workloads. When a customer selects an EU region, Customer Content (request prompts and model outputs) is processed in that region. Access to Customer Content is restricted to authorized personnel on a need-to-know basis and is logged. Certain limited operational metadata (e.g., account and billing information, service health telemetry) may be processed or stored outside the selected region as part of operating and securing the service. For stricter residency requirements (including customer-managed networking and data boundary controls), Redwood offers Dedicated capacity in-region and Private (VPC/on‑prem) deployments.”

Daniel Carter: + a compliance note: we should add “Details available under NDA / via the evidence request process.” Do not attach internal diagrams in email.

Daniel Carter: Also, for “contractual commitment” requests: route to Legal + GRC. We can say “We can evaluate specific contractual residency requirements; please share the questionnaire language.”

Lukas Meyer: Can we explicitly address the “failover” question? They’re worried about cross-region fallback.

Marcus Lin: approved approach: “We route based on the region selected; cross-region routing of customer content is not the standard behavior for region-pinned setups.” Don’t use “never.” If they require a hard guarantee, push to Dedicated/Private + contract language.

Naomi Feldman: Also don’t conflate “data residency” with “data retention.” They’ll ask “how long do you store prompts/logs in EU.” That’s a separate set of knobs depending on deployment.

Avery Johnson: Can you list the top “DON’T SAY” bullets? Helps AEs avoid stepping on landmines.

Olga Petrov: DON’T SAY / avoid:
- “We guarantee EU-only storage for all data, including logs/metadata” (too broad; depends on category + contract)
- “We never route outside the EU under any circumstances” (absolute)
- “We have ISO 27001 certification” (unless explicitly true; use “alignment” language only if approved)
- “Only EU employees can access your data” (personnel location commitments aren’t part of standard controls)
- “We can meet GDPR/Schrems II by default” (legal conclusion; we can describe controls + DPA)

Olga Petrov: DO SAY:
- “EU region selection / region-pinning for Customer Content processing”
- “Access is least-privilege, audited, and requires authorization”
- “Dedicated/Private options for stricter residency boundaries”
- “We can review specific contractual requirements with Legal/GRC”

Priyom Das: They asked if prompts are used to train models. Can we include that line or keep scoped?

Marcus Lin: You can include the standard line if it comes up, but keep it separate from residency so it doesn’t read like a new promise. Approved phrasing is “We do not use Customer Content to train Redwood-provided models unless the customer explicitly opts in under a separate written agreement.” (Don’t improvise.)

Stephanie Nguyen: For live deal: recommend we send the snippet + link the canonical residency/retention overview in Confluence, and offer a quick security call w/ GRC. @Hannah Schmitt I can help coordinate.

Hannah Schmitt: super helpful. Can someone drop the Confluence link name so I don’t grab the wrong doc?

Daniel Carter: Use the customer-facing page in the Security & Compliance space: “Data retention and deletion overview” (has residency notes). If they want SOC 2 report / evidence, follow the “SOC 2 customer evidence request playbook” and ensure NDA is in place.

Lukas Meyer: :thumbsup: I’ll reuse the snippet for my EMEA deal too.

Hannah Schmitt: Thanks all. I’ll reply to the prospect with the approved paragraph + “we can evaluate stricter residency / contractual needs via Dedicated/Private” and pull Legal/GRC if they push on absolute EU-only + EU-only personnel. :white_check_mark:

Jordan Blake: +1 on escalation. If this is a gating item, loop me in early so we don’t burn cycles. Use the approved wording only—no custom commitments in email.

Hannah Schmitt: ack. looping you + @Stephanie Nguyen on the thread w/ the account notes.

title: #marketing: 1768930200
preview:
Ben Carter: putting together the FINAL sprint readout (Week 4) today. need inputs by 3pm PT so Camila can review before we share tomorrow.

Ben Carter: doc will follow the standard template + 1-slide exec summary. pls drop:
1) what shipped (links)
2) results (best estimate + caveats)
3) decision (keep/iterate/kill)
4) follow-ons

Ben Carter: draft readout doc (WIP): https://drive.google.com/file/d/1lifecycle-growth-final-readout-wip
Ben Carter: funnel dashboard: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/activation-funnel-q1

Tyler Benson: for paid/attribution section, here’s the Week 3–4 rollup (post-UTM cleanup):
- Spend cap held: ~$18.5k total (under $20k)
- Best audience: signup-no-activation (1–7d lookback)
- CPA to “first successful request”: ~12–15% lower vs broad site visitors (varies by platform)
- Creative: “Ship your first request in 5 minutes” headline consistently beat the “latency/cost” one on CTR

Tyler Benson: caveat: attribution is directional; we’re relying on UTMs + last-touch in HubSpot. will add in readout as such.

Sergio Costa: dashboard note: numbers look stable after the dedupe fix (PR-28521). I pulled a snapshot for the readout timeframe (Jan 14 → Feb 7) and saved panels here: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1activation-dashboard-screenshots

Sergio Costa: biggest shifts:
- signup → api_key_created improved slightly after console nudge rollout
- api_key_created → first_success improved most from email v2 cohort

Paula Mendes: +1, but please include the footnote: pre-fix (before Jan 24) signup and first_success were inflated in one region due to duplicate event emission. after fix we re-baselined. if anyone asks “why did activation drop overnight”, that’s why.

Paula Mendes: proposed metric phrasing for readout:
- Primary: % of new signups reaching first_success within 7d
- Secondary: % reaching 100k_tokens within 7d, TTFT (p50), D7 active usage

Paula Mendes: also: we should call out that D30 is not fully mature for later cohorts yet, so it’s trend-only.

Marissa Cole: console nudges section draft:
Shipped:
- new nudge component + targeting/holdout (behind `lifecycle_growth_nudges_v1`)
- 4 nudge variants tested: create API key, run first request, enable streaming, view usage dashboard
Results:
- CTR strongest: API key + First request (streaming was meh; usage dashboard got clicks but low downstream)
Decision:
- propose graduating API key + First request nudges to always-on onboarding for self-serve Hosted API
- keep streaming + usage dashboard behind flag for iteration (copy + placement)

Marissa Cole: link to spec: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/PROD/Console+Onboarding+Nudges+Framework

Dylan Brooks: eng addendum: dismissal persistence bug is fixed (the “appears on wrong page” edge case). flag targeting is now by cohort assignment + region guard.

Dylan Brooks: for readout, suggest we explicitly state holdout: 10% holdout for nudges, 5% holdout for email (so we can keep learning without stopping everything).

Soojin Lee: messaging notes for final narrative:
- lead with “get to first working request fast” (time-to-value)
- then “predictable latency + cost” as the reasons to stick around
- avoid over-claiming “fastest” or hard promises in lifecycle touches; use “optimized for production” / “Redwood-verified profiles”

Soojin Lee: also recommend standardizing “first successful request” wording across email + console + landing pages (some variants said “first API call”). small thing but it shows up in support questions.

Tessa Morgan: creative / assets status:
- email template refresh shipped (mobile spacing + CTA button styles). DES-311 closed.
- retargeting kit shipped (smaller “developer-first” variant set requested in thread 1768680044). exports are in the same Drive folder.

Tessa Morgan: if we want a follow-on experiment: add a single graphic/diagram in Email 2 (API key → hello world → usage dashboard) — could reduce confusion for folks who haven’t done this before.

Jordan Blake: reminder for readout: call out the retargeting guardrails + what we DIDN’T do.
- excluded active opps + enterprise domains (weekly refresh)
- excluded regulated segments where tracking consent is unclear
- no targeting of known POCs

Jordan Blake: sales feedback: fewer “why am I seeing ads while in procurement” pings than last quarter. please keep that sentence in there so we don’t regress.

Camila Reyes: for the exec summary, I want 3 bullets:
1) what moved the primary KPI (and by how much, even if it’s a range)
2) what we’re making always-on vs stopping
3) the top 3 follow-ons + resourcing ask (if any)

Camila Reyes: and make the caveats explicit (event fix, D30 maturity). we should be proud but precise.

Ben Carter: super helpful, thx all.

Ben Carter: here’s the structure I’m putting in the readout (so you know what I’ll quote):
- What shipped (email v2 + console nudges + retargeting + instrumentation/dashboard)
- Results summary (activation + TTFT + early retention signals)
- Guardrails status (deliverability / opt-out / audience exclusions)
- Decisions
- Follow-ons

Ben Carter: preliminary “decision” draft (pls react if you disagree):
KEEP:
- Email v2 winner variant for new signups (and expand to stalled-users segment next)
- Always-on console nudges: API key + First request
ITERATE:
- Streaming nudge (placement + better proof/benefit)
- Retargeting: focus budget on signup-no-activation + tighten creative to “first request”
KILL/PAUSE:
- broad site visitor retargeting (too noisy + worse CPA)

Ben Carter: follow-ons I’m listing (top candidates):
1) stalled-user rescue path (no api_key in 24h / no first_request in 72h) — email + console
2) onboarding checklist v1 (2–3 steps max) in console, tied to same events
3) landing page/quickstart alignment (make the “hello world” snippet the canonical CTA everywhere)

Ben Carter: if anyone has strong opinions on order/owners, reply here. otherwise I’ll finalize and send to Camila for review at ~3:30pm PT.

Tyler Benson: +1 on pausing broad visitor retargeting. I’ll add a short note on why (attribution noise + higher support risk).

Paula Mendes: decisions look right. I’ll drop the exact metric definitions + the “post-dedupe” rebaseline note into the doc.

Marissa Cole: ordering works. I can own the checklist follow-on (product) if we keep it lightweight + behind a flag.

Camila Reyes: approved direction. just make sure the exec slide has numbers in ranges and the caveat line. :thumbsup:

Ben Carter: awesome. locking by EOD. thanks everyone.

title: #marketing: 1768672201
preview:
Tyler Benson: Need to lock UTM conventions + landing page mapping for the retargeting launch. Last chance before I bake into HubSpot + ad platform templates.

Ben Carter: yep let’s do it. biggest thing: keep campaigns readable in dashboard + avoid 17 variants. what’s your proposed schema?

Tyler Benson: Proposal (consistent across LinkedIn/Google/Reddit):
- `utm_source`: `linkedin` | `google` | `reddit`
- `utm_medium`: `paid_social` (LI/Reddit) | `paid_search` (Google)
- `utm_campaign`: `ri_retgt_q1_2026_activation` (one umbrella)
- `utm_content`: `{audience}-{creative}-{cta}` (no spaces, dashes ok)
- `utm_term`: only for Google (keyword)
Also add `utm_id` optional later; not required for v1.

Soojin Lee: +1 on readable. Can we standardize audience names so they match our internal segments? i.e., `signup_no_activation` vs `signup-no-activation` etc.

Tyler Benson: Yep. Canonical audience slugs:
1) `site_visitor` (docs + marketing site)
2) `signup_no_activation` (signed up, no first successful request)
3) `activated_no_return` (first success, no activity in 7d)
Creative slugs: `perf` (latency), `cost`, `reliability`, `devex`.
CTA slugs: `quickstart`, `console`, `pricing`.
Example: `utm_content=signup_no_activation-devex-quickstart`

Camila Reyes: Works. Keep `utm_campaign` stable so we can roll up spend/CPA. If we need sub-campaigns later we can do it with `utm_content`.

Jordan Blake: Quick check: landing pages must NOT send enterprise prospects to “Talk to sales” flow via retargeting. We said guardrail is activation-first + exclude active opps.

Tyler Benson: Yep landing page mapping below (activation-first). Also I’ll add exclusions via CRM list + domain suppression. Isabelle — can you confirm the exclusion list format is ready weekly?

Isabelle Laurent: Yes. Weekly export (active opps + “do not market” accounts) to the suppression list. I’ll send you the automation spec after this thread.

Ben Carter: landing pages: can we keep it to 2 pages max? attribution gets messy when we split too much.

Tyler Benson: Proposed mapping:
- Audience `site_visitor` -> `/docs/quickstart` (with “Hello world” snippet + sign-up CTA)
- Audience `signup_no_activation` -> `/console/api-keys` (deep link; if not logged in, redirects to login/signup then back)
- Audience `activated_no_return` -> `/console/usage` (show usage + “enable streaming” nudge + examples)
If we need a non-console option for `site_visitor` on mobile, fallback to `/docs/quickstart?ref=retgt`.

Tessa Morgan: Design note: if we’re sending to `/console/api-keys`, make sure the first screen has context for new users (not just empty state). Otherwise ads will look “broken”. We can add a small top banner behind a flag.

Ben Carter: +1. We have the nudge component already; can reuse with copy like “Create an API key to make your first request”.

Soojin Lee: Copy consistency: for `site_visitor` ads landing on docs quickstart, we should mirror phrasing: “First request in <5 minutes” + “predictable latency/cost”. Avoid “enterprise-grade” wording in these.

Tyler Benson: Great. Locking conventions:
- `utm_campaign=ri_retgt_q1_2026_activation`
- `utm_content={audience}-{creative}-{cta}` using the slugs above
- Landing pages per mapping above (console deep links for post-signup audiences)
I’ll update the retargeting sheet + HubSpot tracking + send final template links.

Camila Reyes: Approved. Tyler please post the final “UTM + LP mapping” in the launch brief thread too.

Tyler Benson: Will do. :thumbsup: (and I’ll add validation in the dashboard so any weird UTMs get flagged)

Ben Carter: :shipit:

title: #partnerships: 1768535122
preview:
Maya Patel: Quick q for growth folks — we’re lining up a Feb partner push w/ Cloud marketplaces (AWS + GCP listings) + 1 co-marketed webinar. If we’re running retargeting at the same time, are we coordinating audiences/UTMs so attribution doesn’t get messy?

Ben Carter: yeah good catch. Right now retargeting plan is (1) site visitors, (2) signup-no-activation, (3) activated-no-return. If partner traffic is hitting the same landing pages, we’ll end up with mixed UTMs + multi-touch confusion.

Tyler Benson: +1. We need to decide: (a) isolate partner landing pages (no retargeting pixel audiences off them), or (b) keep pixels but enforce strict UTM taxonomy + attribution rules (first-touch vs last-touch) so we can still measure.

Maya Patel: partner team preference is to keep partner reporting clean (they’ll want “influenced pipeline” for marketplace + webinar). But I also don’t want to kneecap retargeting if it’s our best activation lever.

Ben Carter: we can do a compromise:
- Dedicated partner LPs: /lp/aws-marketplace, /lp/gcp-marketplace, /lp/partner-webinar
- Retargeting audiences exclude visitors whose last session had `utm_medium=partner` or `utm_source` in {aws_marketplace, gcp_marketplace, partner_webinar}
- Keep generic homepage/docs traffic eligible for retargeting

Tyler Benson: that works. Also: if the partner campaigns are driving *new* signups, they’ll fall into “signup-no-activation” audience unless we suppress. Do we want to suppress them too?

Maya Patel: I think yes for the first 2 weeks? Otherwise partner will claim conversions but retargeting will “close the loop” and get last-touch.

Jordan Blake: from Sales side: please don’t retarget accounts that are coming in through a marketplace deal motion if we’re already engaging them. Similar to the “active opp exclusion” rule. We can treat marketplace-sourced inbound as “sales assisted potential” and suppress from generic ads.

Ben Carter: ok. so:
1) Suppress any domain on active opp list (already in plan)
2) Add suppression for partner/mktplace-sourced signups for N days
Question is N = 14? 30?

Tyler Benson: I’d do 30-day suppression for partner-sourced signups for retargeting *ads* (email lifecycle still ok). That gives partner attribution time + avoids annoying them with ads right after a webinar.

Isabelle Laurent: I can help implement a “partner_sourced” flag in HubSpot / data export so we can build the suppression list. Need a clear definition of partner-sourced:
- any session w/ `utm_medium=partner` OR
- landing page path starts with `/lp/aws-marketplace` etc OR
- referrer matches marketplace domains?

Tyler Benson: define it via UTMs + LP path. Referrers are unreliable. For marketplaces we should force UTMs in the listing CTA and any co-marketing emails.

Maya Patel: we can enforce UTMs in partner assets. For AWS marketplace listing, the CTA link is configurable — we’ll set:
`utm_source=aws_marketplace&utm_medium=partner&utm_campaign=q1_2026_marketplace_push&utm_content=listing_cta`
Similarly for GCP.

Ben Carter: cool. For webinar, we’ll set `utm_source=partner_webinar&utm_medium=partner...`.

Tyler Benson: also we should add a separate retargeting campaign bucket for partner traffic *later*, so we can re-engage them with partner-aligned creative (e.g., “deploy in your VPC / dedicated capacity”) without contaminating the main lifecycle experiments readout.

Camila Reyes: agree w/ separation. Guardrail: the sprint goal is activation lift for Hosted API self-serve. Partner/marketplace is a different motion. Keep reporting separate so we don’t declare victory based on partner-driven intent.

Ben Carter: :thumbsup: action items:
- Maya: confirm partner LPs + UTM conventions with partners
- Tyler: update retargeting-audience-definitions doc w/ partner suppression rules + 30-day window
- Isabelle: confirm we can export “partner_sourced” list weekly for ad platform exclusions
- Ben: update sprint brief + measurement notes to explicitly exclude partner campaigns from primary experiment attribution

Jordan Blake: can you also add “marketplace inbound” to the exclusion category we review weekly? Similar to enterprise opp suppression.

Tyler Benson: yes. We’ll add a weekly sync line item: “new partner-sourced domains + marketplace inbound → exclude from retargeting.”

Maya Patel: perfect. I’ll drop the list of planned partner pushes + dates in this thread once final.

Ben Carter: thanks for catching this early. Would’ve been a mess in Week 3 readout otherwise.

Tyler Benson: +1. Also reminder: for the ad platforms, we need consistent `utm_campaign` prefixes. Propose:
- `lgx_ret_...` for lifecycle growth retargeting
- `partner_...` for partner pushes
Then we can filter cleanly in dashboards.

Maya Patel: Works. I’ll align w/ Partnerships comms team.

Isabelle Laurent: ping me when UTM strings are final; I’ll update the HubSpot tracking rules + property mapping.

Jordan Blake: :eyes: on the suppression list implementation. Don’t want any “active opp” seeing generic signup ads.

Ben Carter: will do. Closing loop once Tyler updates the doc + I push the change to the retargeting ticket.

title: #eng-ml: 1768504321
preview:
Alex Chen: Quick async check — SparrowX v1 weights uploaded to s3://redwood-models/sparrowx/v1/weights.tar.gz. Can someone confirm checksum+size and that the tokenizer vocab matches the packaged tokenizer.json? We hit a weird split on toolcall start token during local smoke. Need this before I kick off canary tomorrow morning.

Priya Nair: I can run checksum now. Is there a weights.sha256 next to the tarball?

Alex Chen: Yes, weights.sha256 is in the same prefix. You can do: `aws s3 cp s3://redwood-models/sparrowx/v1/weights.sha256 - | sha256sum -c -` 

Jordan Blake: Ran the checksum + quick inspect — file matches, size ~26.1GB. Also I reproduced the tokenizer quirk: with sentencepiece and nbest=0 some of the tool-start markers get split when followed immediately by punctuation. Opened PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/models/pull/682 with a tokenizer config change (normalize=true + explicit joiner token).

Sanjay Patel: Minor config catch — config.yaml currently has `model.max_seq: 1024` but the weight pack includes 2048 position embeddings. We should set `model.max_seq: 2048` or runtime will silently truncate longer contexts. Also consider setting batch_policy: latency for the canary to keep tail latencies predictable.

ci-bot: Preflight results for sparrowx/v1: unit tests (tokenizer, kvcache): PASS. Golden-regression: 3 failures. Failing suite: toolcall-calc.integration. Report: /ci/reports/sparrowx/17685 (link). :warning:

Alex Chen: OK noted. Priya — can you re-run the golden prompts focusing on toolcall sequences and structured-output checks? Sample golden prompt we use below (expect strict JSON with fields action + args):

```
System: You are a tool-enabled assistant. When a tool is needed return the tool call in exact JSON.
User: Calculate the sum of 42 and 17 using the calculator tool.
```

Expected tool call: `{"tool":"calculator","action":"add","args":{"a":42,"b":17}}`

Priya Nair: On it. I'll run the golden set + the structured-output linter (rwl-lint) and post results here. If there's trailing commas or stray comments we fail the strict JSON check.

Jordan Blake: FYI if the calculator toolcall keeps failing in the canary, we should add a fallback route to a gpt-compat variant for calculator-heavy traffic. Quick rollout snippet below for the policy file: 

```
rollout:
  primary: sparrowx:v1
  fallback:
    - model: gpt-compat:latest
      condition: "route.reason == calculator_timeout"
```

Sanjay Patel: Also important — the special token `<|toolstart|>` must exist in both the tokenizer vocab and the tool-wrapping layer (tool-runner). If only one side has it, tokenization remapping will break toolcall detection. I added a tiny runtime shim that inserts the token id when tool invocation starts.

Priya Nair: Golden run complete: 42/45 passing. The 3 failing cases were calculator chains where the model emitted trailing commas in the JSON (e.g. `{"action":"add", "args":{...},}`). I pushed a small postprocess step to strict_json in the eval harness and re-ran — all three now pass locally. Pushed results to /ci/reports/sparrowx/17685/golden_v2.json.

Alex Chen: Awesome. Summary of action items: (1) Priya confirmed checksum + golden fixes, (2) Jordan's tokenizer PR merged + adjust sentencepiece settings, (3) Sanjay updated config.yaml max_seq to 2048 and added latency batch_policy, (4) added strict_json postprocess for golden checks, (5) rollout.yaml includes fallback route. I'll schedule canary tomorrow 09:00 PT and tag eng-oncall.

eng-oncall (Bob Li): Acknowledged. I'll monitor traces and SLOs during the canary window. Please add a full health endpoint to the manifest: `/_health?full=true` and enable trace sampling to 10% for the canary.

Alex Chen: Will do — adding healthcheck & sampling now. Thanks everyone :raised_hands:

Priya Nair: Leaving the logs/summary here: checksum ok, tokenizer config adjusted, golden re-run ✅. If anything else pops up ping me @here.

Jordan Blake: noted — I'll watch the canary metrics and have a quick rollback plan in the rollout.yaml.

Sanjay Patel: One last reminder: update the model card with maximum context and tokenizer caveats so downstream teams know about the special token requirement.

Alex Chen: Good call. I'll update model_card.md after the canary goes green. End of thread.

title: #sales: 1768451123
preview:
Avery Johnson: seeing the same security Qs pop up again this week. can we get a canonical list + where in the FAQ to point? dumping what I got from 3 prospects:
- “Do you train/fine-tune on our prompts or outputs?”
- data retention: “how long do you keep prompts/logs? can we set to 0?”
- EU residency: “can you guarantee all data stays in EU? what about support access?”

- ISO: “are you ISO27001 certified?”

title: #product: 1765554321
preview:
Alex Chen: Quick proposal — for Q3 OKRs I want to consolidate embeddings + rerank into a single 'Retrieval' slice to reduce shipping overhead. Key asks: one rollout, unified telemetry, shared caching. Acceptance criteria I’m proposing:
1) end-to-end p95 latency < 250ms for typical 1k-token rerank workloads
2) embeddings 99th < 60ms for small-vector calls
3) regression delta < 1% on our 10 seed customer prompt sets
4) canary + instant fallback to previous model/version.
Thoughts? tagging @eng-runtime @sales

Maya Patel: I like consolidation for developer velocity, but worried about a cost spike for high-throughput rerank. We should bake in cost guardrails: autoscale ceilings + a soft-degrade (approx-match fallback) when cost thresholds hit.

Samir Rao: +1 to cost guards. Also need per-route batching hints and a required quantization plan. Suggest we require a quant profile and a benchmark script in the RFC/PR.

Jordan Blake: From Sales: three enterprise customers ask for per-tenant logs and an SLA on rerank. That pushes toward offering a dedicated-capacity routing option for those tenants — so we can’t do a single-hosted-only rollout unless we support per-tenant routing.

ProductBot: Automated note: RFC placeholder created — https://redwood.internal/rfcs/673 — reviewers: @eng-runtime, @product.

Alex Chen: OK — counter-proposal: do a phased rollout. Phase A: unified API surface + telemetry + opt-in per-customer dedicated routing (so enterprises can pin). Phase B: cost/autoscale optimizations, cache/kv sharing, and single unified backend. Phase A launch criteria: telemetry live, canary run with 10% traffic, 1 enterprise onboarded to opt-in routing, and no regression >1% on eval set.

Maya Patel: Phase A sounds reasonable if we enforce concurrency caps and budget alerts on the canary. Add an ops checklist (OOM monitoring, eviction policy, cache hit dashboards).

Samir Rao: I'll wire up the benchmark harness and include sample runs in the RFC. Will add a required `bench/emb-rerank.sh` that emits p95/p99 and cost-per-req numbers. Canary config snippet I’ll add to PR:
```
canary:
  percent: 10
  route: rerank-v2
  fallback: rerank-v1
```

Jordan Blake: Sales OK with phased approach but needs a timeline for enterprise onboarding — target: enterprise onboarding within 6 weeks after Phase A. Also request: simple docs for POST params and per-tenant logging toggle.

Alex Chen: Great — I’ll author the RFC with the phased acceptance criteria and ping @eng-runtime and @sales next week. TL;DR: consolidate slices for Q3, ship phased rollout, include cost & SLA guards. :thumbsup:

ProductBot: Reminder: Weekly roadmap sync Thu 10:00am — add this RFC to the agenda.

title: #partnerships: 1760265020
preview:
Jordan Blake: tossing an idea in here — can we tie the first public case study drop to a joint webinar w/ a partner?

Two angles:
1) cloud marketplace partner (listing + “how to buy Redwood via marketplace + Dedicated/Private options”)
2) SIEM integration partner (security story: audit logs -> SIEM, SOC workflows)

Feels like this could help enterprise pipeline if we time it w/ the launch. Anyone already talking to partner marketing on either?

Camila Reyes: yes please. we’re trying to stack distribution on top of the case study launch anyway.

q: which customer story are you thinking? Northwind? Helios? (Helios would map cleaner to SIEM imo but approvals are… spicy)

Jordan Blake: exactly. SIEM angle probably maps to Helios (VPC + audit logs). marketplace angle maps to Northwind (Dedicated, predictable spend) or even Atlas if they want procurement-y messaging.

Soojin Lee: +1 but we need to be careful on claims.
- for SIEM: we can say “audit log export + integrations” but not imply out-of-the-box connectors unless we actually have them / partner confirmed.
- for marketplace: avoid “1-click deploy everywhere” style language.

If we do webinar, I’d like to own the abstract + talk track so it stays in our approved pillars.

Markus Klein: from SE side: I can do a “what the architecture looks like” segment (high level). but if Helios is involved we should expect them to request anonymization / no detailed network diagrams.

Also: partner will want to show an integration diagram. we can provide a sanitized version.

Tyler Benson: operationally this is doable if we pick a date soon. i’ll need:
- partner name + co-marketing terms (are they emailing to their list? are we getting lead share?)
- landing page owner (webinar reg)
- UTM plan so we can attribute to pipeline influence

Isabelle Laurent: +1 on lead share. if partner is generating registrations, we need fields + routing rules. if we don’t get emails back we can only count “influence” not sourced.

Avery Johnson: selfishly: marketplace angle could be nice for a couple deals in-flight where procurement is dragging. “we’re on marketplace” is an unlock.

But if we do SIEM angle, that’s a strong late-stage trust builder too (security teams love seeing workflows).

Rachel Kim: quick CS flag: if we’re involving a customer speaker, we need to limit load + set expectations (1 rehearsal + 1 live + 1 optional recording review). we’ve already been cautious about not over-asking.

Miguel Santos: agree w/ Rachel. also: if Helios, their compliance team will want to see slides in advance and will likely request to remove any hard metrics.

Stephanie Nguyen: from sales leadership POV, a webinar can work IF:
1) we can re-use recording in outbound + late-stage sequences
2) we have a clear CTA (talk to sales about Dedicated/Private)
3) it doesn’t slip the case study timeline

Jordan Blake: makes sense.

On partner side, I can get intros w/:
- “CloudPort Marketplace” (our marketplace channel contact) — they’ve been asking for a customer story tie-in
- “SentinelForge” (SIEM partner) — they’ve floated co-marketing around audit log integrations

No promises on lead share yet.

Camila Reyes: for timeline: our target is to publish first story late in the push (week 7–9). if we do webinar, I’d rather schedule it ~1 week after publish so we can point to the live page.

Also: customer consent needs to explicitly cover co-marketing + partner logo appearing alongside (or at least “partner name mentioned”). Legal will ask.

Soojin Lee: +1000. please loop Michael Grant/Sofia Mendes once we pick the option. partner co-branding has weird edge cases.

Markus Klein: Q: do we actually have a named “SIEM integration” today or is it more like “webhook export / S3 export that customers forward to SIEM”?

Don’t want to overstate.

Jordan Blake: mostly the latter. SentinelForge has an ingestion recipe; they want to present it as “supported pattern.”

Soojin Lee: then we should say “validated integration pattern” / “reference architecture” not “native integration.”

Tyler Benson: if we go marketplace angle, who’s the customer? Northwind seems easiest given we already have momentum there.

Camila Reyes: Northwind is the most likely to go public first. Helios is still negotiating compliance redlines.

Maybe: webinar = marketplace + Northwind (safer). then later we do a security-focused session once Helios is cleared.

Avery Johnson: that’s my preference. keep first one simple.

Miguel Santos: Northwind is generally cooperative but they’re picky about logo placement + review windows. if partner logo is on the same slide/page, they’ll want to approve.

Rachel Kim: and we should confirm they’re ok being positioned adjacent to the marketplace partner (some customers hate looking like they’re “endorsing” another vendor).

Jordan Blake: ok.

Action proposal:
1) I’ll ask CloudPort Marketplace for a co-marketing brief: what they’ll do (email, social, event listing), what we get back (lead list or not), date options.
2) Camila/Miguel: can you sanity check w/ Northwind if they’d be open to a webinar *in principle* (no commitment) + whether they’re ok with co-branding w/ marketplace partner?
3) Soojin: draft a 3-sentence abstract + 3 proof points we can safely claim.
4) Tyler/Isabelle: define required form fields + UTM + HubSpot tagging (so we can report).

Camila Reyes: :thumbsup: works. ping me when you have partner constraints so we can slot into distribution calendar.

Soojin Lee: I’ll draft something today. will keep it to performance economics + predictable capacity + procurement friction reduction (via marketplace) and avoid deep metrics.

Markus Klein: if Northwind is on, I can present a “Dedicated deployment overview” without showing exact sizing. I’ll also prep a safe architecture slide we can reuse on the case study page.

Tyler Benson: re: tracking — please ask partner if they can include UTMs on their promo links. if not, we’ll have to use a separate “partner invite” reg link.

Isabelle Laurent: and confirm if they require using *their* registration platform. if so, we need API export or at least CSV within 24h.

Stephanie Nguyen: also add a slide at end for “how to evaluate Redwood Dedicated/Private” + who to contact. we should route inbound to AEs/SDRs properly.

Jordan Blake: got it. I’ll update thread once I hear back from CloudPort.

Camila Reyes: thanks. keeping this thread as our decision log so it doesn’t get scattered.

Jordan Blake: quick update — CloudPort is open to co-hosting, but they want:
- 45-min webinar + 15-min Q&A
- them on for 10 min to cover marketplace purchase flow
- they *can* send to their enterprise list but lead share is “opt-in” checkbox only

They asked for 2–3 date options in ~3–4 weeks.

Tyler Benson: opt-in checkbox lead share is fine. we should make it explicit on the form (“share my info with CloudPort + Redwood”).

Isabelle Laurent: yep. as long as we get the opt-in field in the export.

Soojin Lee: if they’re on-stage for purchase flow, we need to ensure it doesn’t become a pure procurement webinar. we should keep it anchored to the case study story + outcomes.

Camila Reyes: agree. let’s treat CloudPort segment as “how to streamline vendor onboarding” not “buy now.”

Miguel Santos: i’ll check with Northwind on comfort level w/ partner present + recorded session.

Rachel Kim: pls also confirm Northwind speaker capacity. one person is better than multiple.

Markus Klein: I can cover the technical part if Northwind wants to stay lighter. (e.g., Northwind does 5 min context + quote, not a full technical deep dive)

Avery Johnson: +1. if customer is nervous, we can keep them to “why we chose Redwood + what improved” and have Markus do architecture.

Jordan Blake: ok. i’ll hold dates until we hear from Northwind.

Camila Reyes: if Northwind says yes, we’ll lock dates + start the approval checklist (slides > customer review > legal > partner review). keep me posted.

Marta Ruiz: Draft body v0.1:
"Hi —

Earlier today our inference API experienced elevated latency for some customers between ~03:12–03:42 UTC. We resolved the issue by rolling back a routing change that unintentionally directed traffic to a cold GPU pool. During the incident, requests may have experienced timeouts or higher-than-normal response times.

What we know:
- Affected window: ~03:12–03:42 UTC
- Impact: Increased p95/p99 latency and intermittent timeouts for a subset of requests (primarily generation & embeddings).
- Customer impact: Some requests may have been delayed or failed; retries should succeed now.

What we did:
- Identified the routing change and rolled it back at 03:27 UTC.
- Restored normal service by 03:42 UTC and monitored for stability.

Next steps:
- Full RCA and preventative actions to be shared within 72 hours.
- We'll reach out individually to customers who reported failures with mitigation steps and credits where appropriate.

We apologize for the disruption and appreciate your patience.
—The Redwood Inference Team"

Marta Ruiz: Final email draft v1 attached below (short):

Hi [Customer],

If you saw increased latencies or failed requests, please retry; if problems persist, open a support ticket and include any relevant trace IDs. We'll follow up individually if your account was directly impacted.

Best,
The Redwood Inference Team"

- Add metric check to alert on routing imbalance.
Marta Ruiz: Action items (CS):
- Send the short email to impacted customers within 2 hours of this thread closing.
- Post status page update now.
- Offer account adjustments per Legal guidance where remediation is required.

Marta Ruiz: Great. Email ready to go. Sending to impacted customers now and tagging this thread with sent receipts.

preview:
Camila Reyes: It’s live! Benchmark report + interactive page are out in the world 🚀
Links:
• Landing page: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
• PDF: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/report.pdf
• Methodology: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/methodology
Huge thanks to Applied ML + Runtime + Infra + Docs + PMM + Design + Sales Enablement. Drop early feedback / weirdness you see here.

Jordan Blake: Congrats team. This is a big “we can execute cross-functionally” moment. :clap: Please keep an eye out for any community replies/questions we should respond to quickly.

Soojin Lee: +1 and THANK YOU everyone. If you see anyone asking “how did you measure?” please point them to the methodology link above and ping me for anything that needs an official response.

Ethan Wallace: Seeing folks already sharing screenshots of the tables on X. If you spot any confusing chart/tooltips or phrasing, paste the link + quote in-thread and we’ll triage.

Ben Carter: Early funnel numbers (first ~15 min): landing page sessions tracking above forecast. CTA clicks are healthy. Export clicks higher than expected (good sign). Will share a snapshot in #marketing once dashboards settle.

Logan Wright: Confirming analytics events are firing:
- `benchmark_filter_change`
- `benchmark_metric_toggle`
- `benchmark_export_click`
- `benchmark_cta_click`
If anyone sees missing attribution UTM params, send me the URL.

Felix Schneider: Watching the interactive table performance. We’re getting a lot of “filter spam” (people toggling model/region rapidly). Should be fine but if you see slow loads, tell me which browser + region.

Alicia Perez: Design note: mobile is always the risky one for dense tables. If you can, test on your phone and let me know if anything looks broken (especially the disclaimers + tooltips).

Wei Chen: Docs side: links are green on CI and on live. If anyone sees 404s or weird redirects, paste them here.

Jonas Weber: Applied ML: data + methodology are final as published. If you see anyone challenging fairness, we can point to:
- workload definition
- prompt set v3 production mix
- repeated runs + stability gates
We should avoid debating individual point estimates in replies; emphasize distributions/variance + reproducibility.

Claire Dubois: +1. Also reminder: our “latency/$” is normalized; the PDF footnotes explain it. If you see confusion around “why not tokens/sec”, we have an approved answer.

Markus Klein: SE field note: already got 2 inbound pings asking “can I reproduce this in my PoC?” I’m sending the short version: use our harness + same prompt set + same concurrency + caching policy, and expect some regional variance.

Stephanie Nguyen: Got a Slack from an AE: prospect loved the “p95 heatmap” but asked if Dedicated changes the numbers. I told them: same runtime, different capacity isolation + more predictable queueing. Anything else we want to standardize there?

Mateo Alvarez: That’s correct. For public messaging: don’t promise “better latency” on Dedicated; promise predictability/consistency and capacity guarantees. Runtime path is the same.

Lukas Meyer: EMEA folks asking about EU region wording (data residency). The page says “EU regions available” but doesn’t over-specify. Looks good. If someone asks, we should stick to “available regions listed in the filter; talk to sales for residency requirements.”

Naomi Feldman: Quick security scan: public artifacts look clean (no request IDs, no internal cluster names). If you see someone screenshotting raw JSON endpoints, please send me the URL so we can confirm redaction.

Vanessa Ortiz: Infra: traffic is spiky but within reserved headroom. Biggest variable is cache behavior on first load. We’re watching EU + APAC edges.

Sean Gallagher: FYI we saw a brief CDN cache-miss burst right after announcement went out (more origin hits than expected). Not customer-impacting so far. If anyone hears “page slow” from external folks, capture rough timestamp + location.

Neha Kapoor: On-call is staffed for the next few hours. If you see any 5xx/429 chatter, please paste details (screenshots welcome) and we’ll correlate.

inc-bot: Status update: Monitoring alert "Marketing site origin RPS" triggered (warning). Current: 1,820 rps. Threshold: 1,500 rps. Investigating.

Tyler Benson: lol at our own benchmark page getting benchmarked. :)

Ben Carter: Seeing a few reports of “took ~5–8 seconds to load table” from folks in EU. Not consistent. Might be first-load + cache warming.

Felix Schneider: That aligns with what I’m seeing: first paint is fine, but the table data fetch is slower on cold cache. We have a short TTL right now; can bump if needed.

Sean Gallagher: We can tune cache TTL without changing content. If Marketing is okay with slightly less “instant refresh” for the day, we can reduce origin pressure.

Soojin Lee: Yes, please optimize for reliability today. If we need to refresh numbers later, we’ll plan it.

inc-bot: Status update: Mitigation applied: increased CDN cache TTL for benchmark JSON responses. Origin RPS trending down. No incident declared.

Jasmine Liu: CS heads up: Support queue has 3 tickets asking “are these numbers w/ caching?” We’re replying with the methodology link + a short explanation. If we want a one-liner snippet for frontline, send it to me.

Elliot Price: Suggested snippet (approved language from methodology summary): “Results include Redwood’s default production serving settings for this workload, with caching/batching configured as specified in the methodology. We publish the exact configs and rerun thresholds so teams can reproduce and compare.”

Camila Reyes: This thread is exactly why I love this company. Thank you all. Keep dropping feedback + any external questions you see.

Jordan Blake: +1. And remember: be humble in public replies. Lead with methodology + caveats, not victory laps.

Ethan Wallace: Noted. Also, if you see any typos/unclear phrasing, drop a screenshot + location (page section / PDF page #) and we’ll patch.

Alicia Perez: Someone DM’d me: on iPhone Safari the sticky header overlaps the “Disclaimers” accordion when expanded. I’m looking. If anyone can reproduce, please confirm.

Wei Chen: Confirmed on my iPhone 14 / iOS 18.2. It’s minor but real. I’ll file a quick ticket + propose CSS fix.

Felix Schneider: I can ship a quick CSS adjustment today. Ping me the ticket link.

Ben Carter: Last note for now: traffic is still climbing. Biggest source is organic/social, then newsletter. If you’re posting, please use UTM links so we can attribute.

Camila Reyes: Alright, go enjoy the moment for a minute. Then back to watching the dashboards 🙂

title: #eng-security: 1746642007
preview:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off privacy review for PLG signal routing → HubSpot. Need to confirm exactly what fields we’re pushing, retention, and who can see it. @Dr. Aisha Rahman @Michael Grant @Olga Petrov looping you in.

Marcus Lin: Current draft: signal scoring service writes `plg_signal` records to analytics table + pushes a subset to HubSpot Company properties + a timeline note + a task. Question: any blockers on what constitutes “personal data” here?

Logan Wright: From eng side: we can keep it workspace/org-level only. No individual user identifiers needed. The pipeline currently joins on `workspace_id` → `hubspot_company_id` via billing/account mapping.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Good starting point. Let’s be explicit: HubSpot is a CRM and broadly accessible in GTM. Treat anything we send there as “high visibility.” We should avoid raw telemetry, raw URLs, IPs, user agents, individual email/user IDs, and any prompt/content payloads.

Mei Lin: What we have in the aggregator today (internal schema):
- org_id / workspace_id
- window_start/window_end
- signal_type (enum)
- signal_score (0-100)
- signal_confidence (low/med/high)
- counts: tokens_7d, spend_7d, 429_count_7d, doc_views_14d (by category)
- top_model_ids (list)
We planned to send only the “explainable summary” to HubSpot.

Michael Grant: +1 to keeping this non-user level. Also need retention and deletion story. If a customer requests deletion, we should be able to remove HubSpot notes/properties tied to their org.

Marcus Lin: Proposed HubSpot payload (Company-level):
- `ri_plg_last_signal_at` (timestamp)
- `ri_plg_signal_types_30d` (string like “scale, reliability, compliance_intent”)
- `ri_plg_signal_score_30d` (number)
- `ri_plg_signal_summary` (short text)
- `ri_plg_signal_source` (enum: usage_threshold / intent_docs / errors)
- `ri_plg_signal_window` (string: “7d”, “30d”)
- `ri_plg_suppressed_until` (timestamp)
Timeline note: “PLG signal: Reliability risk (429s sustained). Recommend Dedicated eval. Evidence: 429s> X/day for 5 of last 7 days.”
No raw counts unless we agree.

Olga Petrov: For SOC2 + least privilege, we should ensure properties are not editable by everyone (esp. any free-form text fields). Also ensure audit logs exist for who/what wrote the properties.

David Kim: HubSpot supports property-level permissions and can restrict editing/visibility by teams, but it’s not perfect for “security-only.” Usually we can:
- lock properties to Admin write (integration user)
- visibility to Sales/CS only (exclude broader Marketing if needed)
- keep the timeline note short + templated
Also we can keep sensitive details out of notes (notes get copied around).

Tyler Benson: Marketing ask: please don’t drop anything that could be interpreted as “tracking users.” If we do docs intent, keep it category-level and aggregated, not page-level paths, and don’t include query params.

Elliot Price: Docs instrumentation currently captures page category + workspace (if logged in) OR anonymous session if not. For signals, we can require “known workspace” only (i.e., must be authenticated and mapped to an org). And we can bucket pages into coarse categories: Security FAQ, Dedicated docs, Private/VPC docs, Pricing.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Requirement from privacy perspective:
1) Intent signals must be category-level, aggregated, and require a known workspace/org.
2) Do not send the specific doc URL, nor the visitor identity.
3) Minimum threshold before routing (avoid “vanity signal” + reduces creepiness).

Nikhil Sharma: On thresholds: MVP intent signal triggers only if (Security FAQ OR Private deployment docs) viewed ≥3 times over 14d AND org has >N tokens in last 30d OR has added >2 workspaces. Trying to avoid random browsing.

Marcus Lin: Can we include “evidence fields” like “429_count_7d=350” in HubSpot? It helps AEs not ask dumb questions.

Michael Grant: Be careful. Counts themselves aren’t personal, but they are potentially sensitive business data. If included, keep them coarse (ranges) and avoid per-day granularity. Better: “high/medium/low” severity and a short explanation.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Agree with Legal. I’d prefer:
- severity banding (e.g., spend_velocity: high)
- “why” text that is templated and doesn’t include exact numbers
If we must include a number, keep it capped/rounded and ensure it’s only at the company level.

Logan Wright: Easy to do. We can map raw metrics → bands:
- spend_velocity_band: low/med/high
- rate_limit_pressure_band: low/med/high (derived from 429s per request)
- growth_band: steady / accelerating
Then HubSpot note uses those.

Caleb Johnson: Also: make sure we’re not forwarding request IDs, IPs, or error payloads. Some 429 logs include trace IDs that can be correlated. I’ll add a lint/check in the router to block those fields.

SecurityBot: Reminder: Data classification policy applies to CRM exports. Avoid storing customer secrets, tokens, or end-user identifiers in HubSpot. If unsure, default to not sending.

Olga Petrov: Retention: what are we planning for (a) raw events, (b) aggregated dataset, (c) HubSpot properties/notes?

Mei Lin: Current default in analytics:
- raw telemetry events: 30 days
- aggregated daily tables: 13 months (for seasonality)
We can adjust for PLG signals specifically.

Marcus Lin: For HubSpot, I don’t think we can enforce TTL on timeline notes automatically. Properties we can overwrite; notes persist.

David Kim: Correct. Notes are effectively permanent unless manually deleted or via API cleanup job. We can implement a cleanup job keyed on `hubspot_company_id` + a “delete requested” flag, but not true automatic TTL unless we build it.

Michael Grant: Then we should avoid putting anything in notes that we wouldn’t want retained indefinitely. Put details in our internal analytics; in HubSpot keep to a minimal summary + link to internal dashboard (access-controlled).

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That’s the right pattern:
- HubSpot: minimal, non-sensitive summary + routing metadata
- Internal: full signal breakdown behind RBAC
Also ensure we have a documented deletion path for customer requests (DSR).

Jordan Blake: From CRO side: as long as reps can understand “why now” and what to do next, we can live without raw numbers. Don’t slow rollout, but do it clean.

Marcus Lin: Access controls proposal:
- HubSpot properties created under “Redwood PLG Signals” group
- write access: integration user only
- read access: Sales + CS + Sales Ops (exclude broad Marketing by default)
- timeline note template: fixed phrases only, no free-form evidence
Everyone ok?

Tyler Benson: +1 excluding Marketing by default. If we need it later, we can add a sanitized “lifecycle segment” property for nurture.

Olga Petrov: Add to SOC2 evidence: list of fields in/out, retention, and access control configuration. Also document the approval checkpoints.

Marcus Lin: Action items for Confluence data handling page (owner tbd):
1) Enumerate fields sent to HubSpot (ALLOWLIST) + explicit denylist (no PII, no doc URLs, no IPs, no prompts).
2) Retention: raw 30d, aggregates 13mo, HubSpot notes = “no TTL” → keep minimal; properties overwritten; cleanup process for DSR.
3) Access: property permissions (read/write), who can view signal history, audit logs.
4) Deletion request runbook: how to locate company + delete notes/properties + remove from analytics tables.
5) Suppression rules to prevent repeated outreach + opt-out handling.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Assign owners: @Marcus Lin please draft section (1) + denylist. @Mei Lin add retention + dataset storage details. @David Kim add HubSpot permissions + integration user setup. I’ll review and sign off.

Mei Lin: Sounds good. I’ll include the table names + retention configs, plus a note that we only store org/workspace IDs and aggregate windows.

David Kim: I’ll document the HubSpot property group, permission settings, and which teams can read it. Also will add integration audit trail notes.

Michael Grant: Please include a sentence that intent signals are derived from first-party product/docs usage and aggregated at account level; no cross-site tracking; and we provide opt-out if requested.

Nikhil Sharma: I’ll add the “minimum thresholds” and “no vanity signals” rationale to the PRD + link it from the data handling doc.

Marcus Lin: Great. I’ll post draft in Confluence by EOD tomorrow and tag for review. Also I’ll add a unit test in router to enforce the allowlist and fail closed if unknown fields appear.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Final note: for EU accounts, confirm we’re not moving any personal data cross-region. Since this is company-level metadata, should be fine, but document that signals do not include end-user data and are stored per our standard CRM processing terms.

Marcus Lin: Got it. Closing loop once the Confluence page is up + reviewed.

title: #product: 1745123456
preview:
Alex Chen: Heads-up 1 - our DP private preview funnel is underperforming. 50 sent, 3 completed onboarding. We need quick fixes.
Maria Gomez: Saw that. A few people reported the invite form was too long and asked for billing details up front. That likely kills conversion.
Jordan Blake: Also the onboarding checklist references an old role name for service accounts. Confusing and blocks step 3.
Samir Patel: And corporate mail filters are dropping the slack invite link for some domains. Ill add an email fallback with a one-click token.
Alex Chen: Can we split into two outreach paths: fast try (very light touch) vs design partner (commitment asks)?
Maria Gomez: Yes. Fast path = no org questions, single CTA, basic setup doc. DP path = commitment form, NDA, 4-week feedback cadence.
Jordan Blake: Suggest for DP ask we specify per-feature asks instead of vague "feedback": e.g., quality eval on 2 prompts, latency tolerance test, repro steps for any failures.
Samir Patel: Will prototype email fallback and short instructions doc. ETA today EOD. Test cohort id `dp-fast-2025-06-02`.
Iris (eng-bot): deploy: onboarding-docs v2.2 promoted to staging at 2025-06-02T08:12Z
Maria Gomez: PR fixes: `docs/onboard#153` (role names) and `forms/invite#21` (remove org-size mandatory).
Jordan Blake: I'll write an A/B plan for invite copy: baseline vs shorter CTA + incentive (credits + early-access flag). Small test: 100 recipients each.
Alex Chen: Good. Primary KPI = week-1 activation (complete onboarding). Secondary: feedback session attendance, qualitative signal. Goal: lift activation from ~6% -> 25%.
Maria Gomez: New DP acceptance criteria draft:
- attend 4 feedback sessions over 30 days
- provide 3 production-representative prompts/flows
- sign NDA + accept data handling checklist
Jordan Blake: Make feedback sessions 20-30 mins and scoped per feature. Add template questions to make sessions actionable.
Samir Patel: I'll run the quick A/B test with email fallback and track conversions. Will post results in this thread.
Alex Chen: Schedule a 30m sync at 14:00 PST today to triage blockers and decide on rollout. Bring test framework and acceptance copy.
Iris (eng-bot): calendar: DP triage 14:00 PST today 1 attendees: Alex, Maria, Jordan, Samir
Maria Gomez: I'll also add a small incentive line to invite copy: "$500 cloud credits + early feature access" as an experiment.
Jordan Blake: Will prepare updated invite copy and a short list of interview templates.
Samir Patel: Logging cohort: `dp-fast-2025-06-02` in tracker. Will share raw click/open data before sync.
Alex Chen: Action items: Samir (fallback), Maria (docs + invite copy), Jordan (A/B plan + templates). Quick wins first.
Maria Gomez: Adding success metrics to tracker: activation %, onboarding completion rate, feedback compliance (% sessions attended), and week-2 NPS.
Jordan Blake: If conversion improves, we can expand to private preview clients next week.
Alex Chen: Thanks team. Ping me with any blockers prior to the 14:00 sync.
Maria Gomez: :thumbsup:
Samir Patel: on it.
Jordan Blake: :eyes:

title: #docs: 1743412345
preview:
Jordan Blake: Heads-up — production link-check failed after the midday content sync. Error pointing to a missing anchor `#cli-integration` from /docs/quickstart.html.
Jordan Blake: Log snippet: ```Error: Could not find anchor #cli-integration referenced from /docs/quickstart.html: line 78```
Eva Lopez: That page had a heading `## CLI Integration (macOS, Linux)` which I recently changed to `## CLI Integration (macOS / Linux) 🛠️` to be more friendly. Looks like the emoji and slash changed the slug.

title: #finance: 1739123456
preview:
Samir Patel: Quick question — corpcard declined mid-trip in Prague so I covered a client dinner on my personal card. Need approval + VAT reclaim guidance. Details below.

Samir Patel: ```
Merchant: U Menec Restaurant
Date: 2024-10-10
Amount: CZK 3,420 (posted) — charged to personal card (VISA ****1234)
Itemized invoice attached (shows VAT ID and line items).
Purpose: Client dinner with Acme integration team
```

Maria Chen: Thanks — can you confirm: did you get manager sign-off before paying? We need an itemized invoice with the vendor VAT number to attempt reclaim. If you have a photo of the invoice upload that with the claim.

Samir Patel: Manager Jordan Blake approved verbally at the table. tagging him here @Jordan Blake. I uploaded the PDF to the expense thread.

Jordan Blake: I approve — Samir, go ahead and submit as "Out-of-pocket / Travel" and add me as approver in the form.

ExpenseBot: Submit your expense here: https://expenses.redwood.example/submit — category: Out-of-pocket -> Travel. Upload receipt and invoice. Please set "Expense type" = Personal card (company business).

Lee Nguyen: FYI on VAT — to reclaim Czech VAT we need the vendor invoice with the vendor VAT (DIČ) and a clear breakdown of VAT amount. If the invoice has that, we can file the reclaim; if not, we usually reimburse the gross amount but can't recover tax. Also confirm the currency on the invoice (CZK).

Samir Patel: Invoice has vendor DIČ and line-level VAT. Exchange rate on my bank statement: CZK 3,420 = USD 150. I put both amounts in the upload and attached a screenshot of the statement.

Maria Chen: Perfect. I'll route to AP for VAT reclaim. AP will either (1) include it in the monthly EU VAT reclaim batch or (2) if missing fields, mark as non-reclaimable and reimburse gross. Expect ~2-4 weeks for reclaim processing.

Samir Patel: Cool — thanks. Should I put any special note about corporate card blockage in the form?

Jordan Blake: Add a short note: "Corporate card declined — approved to use personal card by manager on site." That should be fine for audit.

Lee Nguyen: Also tag the expense with the project code: ACME-PILOT so cost shows up correctly.

Samir Patel: Done, uploaded with project code and manager note. Thanks everyone :thumbsup:

Maria Chen: Closing loop — AP will ping this thread if they need additional docs.

ExpenseBot: Expense submitted (ID EXP-20241010-789). Current status: Pending AP review.

title: #eng-ml: 1717778000
preview:
Ravi Singh: Quick sync - we need clearer rollback/hysteresis rules for low-bit rollouts. current 'fail fast' reverts are noisy for transient noise.
Olivia Park: Agreed. My proposal: add a hysteresis window so a single spike doesn't auto-revert. e.g., require 3 consecutive windows (1m windows) breaching threshold before revert.
Mei Tan: +1. Also add a cool-down after a revert to avoid flip-flop (e.g., 30m hold).
Jordan Blake: We should separate metrics that trigger immediate safety revert (safety-critical degradation) vs those that are soft (quality delta). Immediate metric: safety_token_mismatch or hallucination_count above X -> immediate revert. Soft metric: human-eval proxy (degradation_score) -> hysteresis.
Ravi Singh: Good. Concrete thresholds suggestion: safety metric -> immediate. quality metric -> breach if degradation_rate > 0.6% for 3 windows. latency -> breach if p95 delta > 5ms for 3 windows.
Olivia Park: Also support per-tenant sensitivity: enterprise customers might set tighter thresholds, small tenants looser. Add tenant_override in profile meta.
Mei Tan: Need to define windowing semantics. suggest sliding windows with step = 30s, window_size = 60s, requiring N consecutive breached windows. That smooths short spikes while still being responsive.
Jordan Blake: For revert action, plan two modes: auto-revert (default for non-safety issues) and gated-revert (requires human ack for safety_critical models/tenants). Controlled by profile metadata: auto_revert: true/false.
Ravi Singh: Add provenance tagging to the revert: include profile_id, signer, canary_percent, triggered_metric, window_summary. Keep it compact in alert payload for postmortems.
Olivia Park: Telemetry impact: we mustn't explode cardinality. We'll store aggregated counters per-deploy + sampled detailed traces for breached windows. Keep per-tenant summaries only when tenant has overrides.
Mei Tan: Playbook: when auto-revert happens create an incident with severity based on metric. For soft reverts, auto-create a rollback ticket but tag for triage. Ops-bot?
ops-bot: Created ML-4401: "Quant rollback hysteresis + playbook" assigned to Ravi Singh, Olivia Park, Mei Tan, Jordan Blake. https://redwood.atlassian.com/browse/ML-4401 :white_check_mark:
Jordan Blake: We need a lint step that prevents profiles from setting auto_revert=true combined with aggressive thresholds for safety_critical ops. Block if unsafe.
Ravi Singh: Good call. Also add a manual "hold" flag to temporarily disable auto-revert for debugging canaries. Admin-only.
Olivia Park: UI needs to show current holograph of hysteresis state per-deploy (OK / warning (1/3) / breached (3/3) / reverted). That helps SREs see progress.
Mei Tan: For postmortems we want a compact event line: "ts, deploy, profile_id, tenant, metric, windows=[0.2,0.7,0.8], action=revert". That makes search easy.
Jordan Blake: How about a graceful degrade path instead of full revert? e.g., step back from fp8->int8 globally instead of full rollback to model-level. That can buy time while ML investigates.
Ravi Singh: I like staged rollback options: revert_to_previous_signed_profile, fallback_level_one (e.g., move to next entry in fallback chain), or full-disable-operator_level. Profile should define preferred rollback modality.
Olivia Park: Security: ensure revert commands are audited and require 2FA for manual overrides on prod.
Mei Tan: Implementation items: 1) runtime: sliding-window aggregator + hysteresis state machine. 2) telemetry: compact window summaries + sampled traces. 3) ML: mapping from degradation_score -> fallback severity. 4) product: UI states and manual hold button.
Ravi Singh: Timeline? want PoC for hysteresis state machine in two weeks.
Jordan Blake: I'll prototype the state machine and staged rollback hooks this week.
Olivia Park: I'll draft the UI states and incident templates.
Mei Tan: I'll implement telemetry windows and sampling.
ops-bot: Added checklist items to ML-4401 and pinged assignees. :memo:
